envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Other Support Activities for Road Transportation

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include municipalities and road agencies, commercial fleets and carriers, terminal users, and infrastructure owners. Revenue ranges from multi-year fixed-price or unit-price service contracts and scheduled recurring work to usage fees and availability-payment or concession arrangements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default lens is narrowed to recurring or repeat outsourced operating services because this code combines equipment-based municipal services, freight-handling services, shared facilities, inspections, and infrastructure concessions whose economics are otherwise incoherent in one operating-company screen.
lens.excluded_activities: Motor vehicle towing and emergency roadside service classified in 488410; captive facilities used only by an owner's vehicles; government-operated road assets; shells, non-control financing vehicles, and businesses without transferable recurring operations.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat outsourced street sweeping, road-transport inspection or weighing, truck loading and unloading, shared terminal or maintenance-facility services, and contracted toll-road, bridge, tunnel, or related road-network operations to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 4884 Support Activities for Road Transportation population, including towing, rather than 488490 alone. Its largest occupation is heavy and tractor-trailer truck drivers (52,190), while dispatchers (8,560), office clerks (4,870), and general and operations managers (4,330) form a smaller information-work layer. O*NET task descriptions are mapped qualitatively to current generative-AI, optimization, document-processing, and voice-agent capabilities; the proxy may understate 488490 exposure if towing drivers dominate the broader group, but may overstate it where safety-critical judgment and field exceptions constrain substitution.
caveats: The occupation mix is reported at NAICS 4884 and includes 488410 motor vehicle towing.
O*NET reports occupation-wide tasks, not time shares or 488490-specific task execution.
The estimate covers not-yet-automated current tasks and excludes autonomous driving or fully robotic field operations that are not plausibly deployable across the lens within five years.
a.change_evidence: A 488490-only OEWS special tabulation joined to O*NET task time shares and payroll by occupation, plus time-and-motion studies from street-sweeping, inspection, terminal, and toll-operations firms, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Most wage-weighted work is physical vehicle operation, material handling, mechanical work, or on-road inspection. Exposed work is concentrated in dispatch optimization, work-order intake, scheduling, customer communications, record maintenance, billing support, compliance documentation, and management analysis. A limited slice of driver paperwork and routing is also exposed, but vehicle operation itself is not counted as near-term AI substitution.

d5.bridge: FHWA projects nationwide VMT rather than demand for 488490 services. Applying its baseline 0.7% annual total-VMT growth over five years gives approximately 1.04; the range reflects FHWA's 0.5%-1.0% annual alternative outlooks plus activity-mix uncertainty. This may overstate demand where virtual inspection or municipal insourcing reduces purchased service and understate it where truck VMT, environmental cleaning standards, congestion, or infrastructure complexity increase service intensity faster than total VMT.
caveats: VMT is an indirect demand driver and not an output measure for 488490.
The code combines services with different exposure to passenger, single-unit truck, and combination-truck mileage.
Municipal fiscal stress, regulatory changes, severe weather, or insourcing can dominate VMT at local firms.
d5.change_evidence: Build activity-weighted five-year forecasts from municipal sweeping bid volumes, weigh/inspection transactions, terminal throughput, lane-miles under private operation, and customer budget plans, separating real volume from price and quality changes.
d5.rationale: Road-network support demand should broadly track road usage, with FHWA forecasting modest growth in total VMT and faster growth for truck classes. Street sweeping and infrastructure operations also depend on municipal budgets, service standards, network mileage, and weather, so constant-price demand is expected to grow only modestly rather than mirror nominal contract spending.

caveats: No source reports eligibility under the frozen lens.
The injected firm count is margin-bridged and may not reflect the code's activity mix.
Government contracting and infrastructure concessions create materially different control and renewal risks.
e.change_evidence: Classify a complete 488490 establishment and firm universe from Census microdata or a commercial business database by activity, ownership, customer concentration, recurring-revenue share, captive status, and transferability, then reconcile establishments to firms.
e.rationale: A majority of LMM-band firms should provide transferable external services, particularly contracted sweeping, handling, inspection, and shared-facility operations. Eligibility is reduced by captive terminal or maintenance facilities, government-controlled infrastructure, concession structures that are not operating-company control transfers, one-off/project revenue, and businesses whose value is inseparable from a personal license or relationship.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 488490 ($2,447,764,171) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 488490 ($6,983,313,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $129,325 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 488490); class <5: 1,781 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,768 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,585 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 281 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,039,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $200,471 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 150 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,671,113 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $360,870 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 142 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,553,570 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $840,816 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 66 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,328,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,506,742 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,667,674 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,767,032 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 112

caveats: There is no measured 488490 operator-required share.
Virtual inspection may materially reduce staffing or station stops without eliminating the accountable operator.
The estimate is for the current service basket and does not credit new digital services.
o.change_evidence: Survey customers and operators by activity on planned insourcing, fully unattended sites, virtual weigh-station substitution, autonomous sweeping, and software-only procurement, then measure actual service-unit displacement in deployments operating for at least 12 months.
o.rationale: The current basket chiefly delivers physical outcomes: cleaning streets, moving or handling vehicles and freight, operating shared facilities, inspecting vehicles, and maintaining accountable road infrastructure. Software, remote sensing, virtual weigh stations, and automation can reduce labor and on-site steps, but customers still need a legally and operationally accountable service operator for assets, safety, exceptions, equipment, and contract performance. Only a small portion of current quantity is likely to be eliminated or fully self-served within five years.

caveats: Public street-sweeping contracts illustrate only one activity inside the code.
Toll concessions and shared-terminal fees may have very different indexation and demand-risk terms.
The estimate excludes implementation difficulty, demand loss, and valuation effects.
q.change_evidence: Review a stratified sample of 50 customer contracts and renewal histories for change-in-law, benchmarking, open-book, CPI, gain-share, termination, and rebid provisions, and compare realized price and margin changes after measurable productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Multi-year fixed-price and unit-price contracts can let an operator retain savings before renewal, particularly when AI reduces back-office staffing or avoids incremental hiring without changing service quality. Competitive rebids, transparent unit pricing, CPI escalators, cost reviews, customer bargaining, and usage-based arrangements progressively share benefits with customers. The discounted five-year base therefore retains a little over half of implemented gross benefit.

caveats: Census AI adoption figures cover all US employer businesses rather than 488490 or the LMM band.
Implementation could be faster for standardized dispatch and clerical work but slower for public infrastructure operations and safety-critical inspection.
This primitive excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain vendor telemetry and before-and-after labor-hour data from 20 or more LMM operators using dispatch, fleet-management, computer-vision inspection, or AI customer-service tools, segmented by activity and implementation vintage.
rho.rationale: Dispatch assistance, call summarization, document extraction, invoicing support, scheduling, route suggestions, and exception triage can be integrated into existing workflows, but fragmented small operators, legacy systems, thin technical staffs, public-contract requirements, cybersecurity, data quality, and safety accountability will prevent full implementation. The five-year base assumes focused workflow redesign captures roughly half of the exposed opportunity, with humans retaining dispatch authority and field-safety decisions.

caveats: The SBA retirement study is old, economy-wide, and based on individuals rather than 488490 firms or observed transfers.
Private small-company transactions are incompletely reported.
Concession changes and public contract awards are not automatically qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Match five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a), state business-registration, and UCC records to a verified 488490 firm universe, distinguishing qualifying control transfers from closures, asset-only sales, internal reorganizations, and non-control investments; supplement with owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: The five-year control-transfer probability reflects normal small-business succession, strategic sales, and sponsor acquisitions, tempered by owners' tendency to retire later than employees and by alternatives such as family succession, continued ownership, closure, or asset liquidation. The base is a bounded judgment rather than an observed transaction rate.

principal_driver: Recurring physical road-support demand paired with dispatch, documentation, scheduling, and billing workflows that can absorb practical AI assistance.
principal_weakness: Severe business-model heterogeneity and competitive public or unit-priced contracts make both firm eligibility and retained economics uncertain.
